content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

April 5, 2017

Steven W. Dahlem
County Counsel
5100 Bullion Street
P.O. Box 189
Mariposa, CA 95338

Re:    Your Request for Informal Assistance
       Our File No. I-17-054

Dear Mr. Dahlem:

     This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of John McCamman, a member of
both the Mariposa County Water Agency Advisory Board and the Mariposa County Grand Jury,
regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note
that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under
other general conflict of interest prohibitions, such as common law conflict of interest, Section
1090, or incompatible offices under Section 1099. We are not a finder of fact when rendering
advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are
complete and accurate. Because your question is general in nature and not limited to specific
governmental decisions, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.²

QUESTION

     Does the Act prohibit Mr. McCamman, a member of both the Mariposa County Water
Agency Advisory Board and the Mariposa County Grand Jury, from additionally holding office as a
member of the Mariposa County Planning Commission?

CONCLUSION

     No. There is nothing in the Act that prohibits an individual from concurrently holding
multiple public offices. However, because laws outside the Act's purview may restrict an official
from holding multiple public offices simultaneously if those offices are determined to be
"incompatible offices," we encourage you to check with the Attorney General's office to determine
if other laws apply to the facts presented.

_______________
     ¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

     ² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal
written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

File No. 1-17-054
Page No. 2

Moreover, although concurrently holding multiple offices does not, in and of itself, create a
potentially disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act, we caution that the Act prohibits
Mr. McCamman from participating in making or attempting to use his official position to influence
the Board of Supervisors' decision regarding the appointment of a planning commissioner for the
supervisorial district in which Mr. McCamman resides.

FACTS

John McCamman is currently a member of both the Mariposa County Water Agency
Advisory Board and the Mariposa County Grand Jury. The County will soon consider the
appointment of a planning commissioner for the supervisorial district in which Mr. McCamman
resides, and Mr. McCamman is interested in additionally serving in that position. Pursuant to
Section 2.52.100 of the Mariposa County Code, the County pays its planning commissioners $50
per meeting and reimburses them for any accrued mileage at the federal rate. You are the Mariposa
County Counsel and the authorized representative of Mr. McCamman.

ANALYSIS

At the outset, it is important to note that the Act's conflict of interest provisions apply on a
decision-by-decision basis and depend heavily on the specific facts of each decision. Therefore, this
letter only provides general guidance. Whether Mr. McCamman has a disqualifying conflict of
interest under the Act with respect to a specific decision must be answered as the decision arises.³

The Act's conflict of interest provisions ensure that public officials will "perform their
duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the financial
interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).) Specifically, Section 87100
prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her
official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest.
Section 87103 provides that an official has a "financial interest" in a decision, within the meaning
of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have material financial effect on one
or more of the official's interests identified in that section. An official always has an interest in his
or her personal finances or those of an immediate family member. (See Section 87103; Mulligan
Advice Letter, No. A-17-023; Snow Advice Letter, No. A-16-268; Muñoz Advice Letter, No. A-15-
219).⁴

_____________

³ You have also asked, if the Board of Supervisors appoints Mr. McCamman to the Planning Commission,
whether the Act would prohibit Mr. McCamman from taking part in Grand Jury decisions relating to an investigation of
either the County's Water Agency Advisory Board or its Planning Commission. As noted above, whether the Act
prohibits a public official from taking part in a decision must be determined as the decision arises. However, generally,
the Act would prohibit Mr. McCamman from taking part in those decisions if they would have a reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect on one or more of his interests. (See Sections 87100 and 87103.)

⁴ Mr. McCamman does not have a source-of-income interest in the County because "[s]alary, reimbursement
for expenses or per diem, and social security, disability, or other similar benefit payments received from a state, local,
or federal agency" does not constitute "income" under the Act. (Section 82030(b)(2).)

File No. I-17-054
Page No. 3

Foreseeability and Materiality

Regulation 18701(a) provides that the effect of a decision before the official's agency is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the official's interest is "explicitly involved" in the decision; an official's interest is explicitly involved in a decision if the interest is a named party in, or the subject of, the decision; and the official's interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the official's interest.

Regulation 18702.5(a) sets forth the materiality standard applicable to a decision's effect on an official's interest in his or her personal finances: "The financial effect is material if the official or the official's immediate family member will receive a measurable financial benefit or loss from the decision."

The County pays its planning commissioners $50 per meeting and reimburses them for any accrued mileage at the federal rate. Therefore, the Board of Supervisors' decision regarding the appointment of a planning commissioner for the supervisorial district in which Mr. McCamman resides would result in a measurable financial benefit or loss to Mr. McCamman. Accordingly, that decision would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on Mr. McCamman's interest in his personal finances.

Making, Participating in Making, or Using Official Position to Influence

Regulation 18704 defines "Making a Decision," "Participating in Making a Decision," and "Using Official Position to Attempt to Influence a Decision," and provides in pertinent part as follows:

"(a) Making a Decision. A public official makes a governmental decision if the official authorizes or directs any action, votes, appoints a person, obligates or commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her agency.

"(b) Participating in a Decision. A public official participates in a governmental decision if the official provides information, an opinion, or a recommendation for the purpose of affecting the decision without significant substantive review.

"(c) Using Official Position to Attempt to Influence a Decision. A public official uses his or her official position to influence a governmental decision if he or she:

"(1) Contacts or appears before any official in his or her agency or in an agency subject to the authority or budgetary control of his or her agency for the purpose of affecting a decision; or

File No. I-17-054
Page No. 4

"(2) Contacts or appears before any official in any other government agency
for the purpose of affecting a decision, and the public official acts or purports
to act within his or her authority or on behalf of his or her agency in making
the contract."

Thus, Mr. McCarmnan cannot "make" the decision at issue because an official who is not a member
of the Board of Supervisors lacks the authority to "appoint a person" to the Planning Commission.

As a member of both the County's Water Agency Advisory Board and its Grand Jury,
Mr. McCamman could potentially "participate in the making" or "use his official position to
attempt to influence" the decision. However, because the decision regarding the appointment to the
Planning Commission would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on his personal
finances, under Regulation 18704(c)(2) above, the Act prohibits him from attempting to use his
official positions at the Water Agency Advisory Board or the Grand Jury to influence, that decision.

Laws Outside of the Act

Although there is nothing in the Act that prohibits an individual from concurrently holding
multiple public offices, laws outside the Act's purview may restrict an official from holding
multiple public offices simultaneously if those offices are determined to be "incompatible offices."
(See Sections 1099 and 1990.) Based on the facts presented, we encourage you to check with the
Attorney General's office to determine if the doctrine of incompatible offices or any other laws
apply.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: Matthew F. Christy
Counsel, Legal Division

MFC:jgl

Does the Act prohibit Mr. McCamman, a member of both the Mariposa County Water 
Agency Advisory Board and the Mariposa County Grand Jury, from additionally holding office as a 
member of the Mariposa County Planning Commission?
sections.conclusion:
No. There is nothing in the Act that prohibits an individual from concurrently holding 
multiple public offices. However, because laws outside the Act's purview may restrict an official 
from holding multiple public offices simultaneously if those offices are determined to be 
"incompatible offices," we encourage you to check with the Attorney General's office to determine 
if other laws apply to the facts presented.2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal 
written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)  
Moreover, although concurrently holding multiple offices does not, in and of itself, create a 
potentially disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act, we caution that the Act prohibits 
Mr. McCamman from participating in making or attempting to use his official position to influence 
the Board of Supervisors' decision regarding the appointment of a planning commissioner for the 
supervisorial district in which Mr. McCamman resides.
